title: Lesson 4: Growing Deep Roots in Jesus
description: The name of this course is Growing Deep, and this is the lesson where the name comes to life. Jesus is not only your good Shepherd, your good Healer, and your good Savior. He is also your Good Soil. The spiritual disciplines are the roots that go down into Him for the rest of your life, and the deeper those roots go, the more of His fruit shows up above the surface.

blocks.text: You have met the Father who runs toward you, the Son who gave you a new identity, and the Spirit who walks with you as your Guide. Those three lessons gave you the shape of God: love, identity, presence. Now you step into the heart of this course. The name Growing Deep is not a slogan. It is the shape of a life with Jesus. Before a plant grows up, it grows down. Before fruit ever shows on the branches, roots are quietly reaching into the soil. This lesson is about the roots.

blocks.alt: A close up view of the four different soils: hard path, rocky ground, thorny ground, and good soil with deep roots

blocks.title: The Sower and the Soil
blocks.text:
Jesus sat in a small boat just off the shore, and the people crowded the beach to hear Him. He told them a story about a farmer and a handful of seed.

"Listen! Behold, a sower went out to sow. And as he sowed, some seed fell along the path, and the birds came and devoured it. Other seed fell on rocky ground, where it did not have much soil, and immediately it sprang up, since it had no depth of soil. And when the sun rose, it was scorched, and since it had no root, it withered away. Other seed fell among thorns, and the thorns grew up and choked it, and it yielded no grain. And other seeds fell into good soil and produced grain, growing up and increasing and yielding thirtyfold and sixtyfold and a hundredfold." And he said, "He who has ears to hear, let him hear." (Mark 4:3-9)

Four soils. Same seed. Same sun. Same rain. The difference was the ground. The hard path never let the seed in. The rocky ground had no depth, so the plant sprang up fast and died faster, because there was no root. The thorny ground let the weeds win. Only the good soil let the roots go down, and the harvest was staggering.

Most people read this parable looking in a mirror, and that is a good place to start. Which soil am I right now? Is my heart hard, shallow, crowded, or open? That question is always worth asking. But there is a deeper reading of this parable that Christians have treasured for centuries, and it is the door into this whole lesson.
Jesus Is the Good Soil

You already know some of the names of Jesus. He is the good Shepherd who finds the one lost sheep and carries it home (John 10:11). He is the good Healer who touches the leper, opens blind eyes, and makes broken bodies whole (Mark 1:41). He is your good Savior who took your sin on the cross and rose again to give you life (1 Timothy 1:15). There is one more name worth holding onto from this parable. Jesus is your Good Soil.

Where your heart was path-hard, rocky-shallow, or thorn-choked, Jesus was the one honest and good heart that heard God's Word perfectly, trusted it fully, obeyed it completely, and produced the hundredfold harvest that you never could. And because you are now in Him, His soil becomes your soil. Your roots do not have to find their own way in hard ground. They grow down into the Good Soil that Jesus already is. The deeper they go, the more of His life you receive.
Therefore, as you received Christ Jesus the Lord, so walk in him, rooted and built up in him and established in the faith, just as you were taught, abounding in thanksgiving.

Paul picks his words carefully. Rooted in Him. Built up in Him. Established in Him. The Christian life is not a better version of you working harder. It is the same you sinking roots into the same Jesus, a little deeper every season. Just as you received Him, so you keep walking in Him.
Growing Deep Before You Grow Tall

The world measures a tree by what it sees above the ground. God measures it by what is happening underneath. A plant whose roots sit near the surface looks fine until the heat comes, and then it withers. A tree whose roots have patiently sunk down into the water table stands through droughts, storms, and summers that flatten everything else. The root work is slow, hidden, and unglamorous, and most of the world will never see it. But it is the reason the tree is still standing when the wind blows through.
4.1 - Which of the four soils best describes your heart in this season, and where do you sense Jesus inviting you to go deeper?
Five Roots That Go Down Into Jesus

Spiritual disciplines are simply the roots. They are not extra credit, and they are not how you earn God's favor. We strive because we are in the family, not to earn our way into it. The disciplines are the slow, ordinary practices that keep drawing your life up out of Jesus, the Good Soil. Five of them show up across the whole Bible, and together they form the root system of a life with Jesus. Walk the tool below and watch each root reach down into the soil, one at a time.
Read each root slowly. Notice which ones already feel like home, and which ones the Spirit nudges you to look at again.

Prayer. The first root is the simplest. Prayer is honest conversation with God, talking and listening. The Father has run toward you, and prayer is how you keep walking down the road home. Jesus taught His disciples to pray with worship, confession, thanks, and requests. Paul tells the church to "pray in the Spirit on all occasions" (Ephesians 6:18). You do not need fancy words. Start with five quiet minutes in the morning, naming where you need Him, and let the rest grow from there.

The Word. The Bible is God's voice to you, and the person whose roots are in Scripture is the one who stays green in a dry year. There are four ways the Bible invites you in: read, study, memorize, meditate. Most days, simply read. The Psalmist says, "I have stored up your word in my heart, that I might not sin against you" (Psalm 119:11). A small amount each day, done faithfully, will form you more than a giant amount done once in a while. Pick one Gospel and read one chapter a day until you have walked through it.

Fellowship. You were not designed to follow Jesus alone. The Christian life has always been a "one another" life: love one another, encourage one another, bear one another's burdens. The writer of Hebrews says, "Let us consider how to stir up one another to love and good works, not neglecting to meet together... but encouraging one another" (Hebrews 10:24-25). Find a church where you can be known. Find one or two believers you can be honest with. The roots that grow tangled with other roots are the ones that survive the storm.

Stewardship. Everything you have belongs to God already. Your time, your talents, and your treasure are not yours to clutch. They are gifts to steward. Paul writes, "God loves a cheerful giver" (2 Corinthians 9:7). Stewardship is not how you earn favor with God. It is how you stop pretending you are the owner. Start by giving something back to God this week, whether it is an hour, a skill, or a portion of your paycheck. Generosity is the soil where greed dies.

Fighting Sin. The Christian life includes a real fight. Not against people, but against the pull of your old self and the lies of the enemy. Paul puts the question bluntly in Romans 6: "What shall we say then? Are we to continue in sin that grace may abound? By no means!" Grace does not excuse sin. Grace kills it. Peter says it the practical way: "abstain from the passions of the flesh, which wage war against your soul" (1 Peter 2:11). You fight sin not by trying harder, but by running to Jesus faster. Confess what you cannot beat alone. Stand against one specific temptation by faith this week, and ask the Spirit who lives in you to walk you through it.

Each of these five roots will return one at a time across the rest of the series. The Word goes deeper across lessons 5 through 7. Stewardship gets a full lesson in 201.10. Fighting sin gets its own lesson in 202.09. Fellowship gets a full lesson in 301.01. Prayer threads through every one of them. This lesson is the high view that lets you see the whole root system at once, so that no single discipline becomes the whole Christian life and none of them gets quietly dropped.
Paul gives the apostolic summary for this whole pattern in Romans 12. Listen to how he describes the shape of a life with deep roots.

I appeal to you therefore, brothers, by the mercies of God, to present your bodies as a living sacrifice, holy and acceptable to God, which is your spiritual worship. Do not be conformed to this world, but be transformed by the renewal of your mind, that by testing you may discern what is the will of God, what is good and acceptable and perfect.

Notice the order. Mercy first, then offering. Salvation first, then sacrifice. Paul does not start with what you must do for God. He starts with what God has already done for you, and then he calls you to offer your whole self in response. The five roots are exactly the shape of that living sacrifice. Prayer offers your voice, the Word offers your mind, fellowship offers your relationships, stewardship offers your time, talents, and treasure, and fighting sin offers your body. Every root is a quiet "yes, Lord" said with one part of your life at a time, until your whole self has been laid on the altar in worship.
4.2 - Be honest. Which of the five roots feels strongest in your life right now, and which one feels the shallowest? What is one reason for the gap?
The Fruit Comes From the Roots

Here is the invitation and the comfort of this whole lesson. You do not have to manufacture your own fruit. You do not have to strain for peace, squeeze out joy, force patience, or white-knuckle your way into love. The fruit shows up on the branches when the roots are drinking from the soil. Jesus said it like this in the upper room:

Abide in me, and I in you. As the branch cannot bear fruit by itself, unless it abides in the vine, neither can you, unless you abide in me. I am the vine; you are the branches. Whoever abides in me and I in him, he it is that bears much fruit, for apart from me you can do nothing.

Abide is a quiet word. It means to stay, to remain, to keep living where you already are. Your roots in Jesus are not something you have to invent. You have been grafted into the Vine. The job is to stay there, to keep drinking, to keep going deeper. The fruit is His to grow, in His time, in the measure He chooses. Thirty, sixty, a hundredfold. That is not your effort producing results. That is the Good Soil doing what only He can do.

Character is the unseen part of a Christian's life. Most of the world will never know how much time you spent praying, how many verses you hid in your heart, how often you stayed faithful in a quiet hour no one noticed. But the fruit will show. A steadiness under pressure. A kindness that outlasts a bad day. A love for people who are hard to love. A slow, real peace that your old life never held. That is the Good Soil speaking through your branches. That is the kind of life Jesus is growing in you, one season at a time, for the rest of your life.
When the Storms Come

Jesus ended the most famous sermon He ever preached with two builders. One built his house on sand. One built his house on the rock. The sun shone on both houses. The rain came on both. The wind blew on both. But only one was still standing in the morning.

Everyone then who hears these words of mine and does them will be like a wise man who built his house on the rock. And the rain fell, and the floods came, and the winds blew and beat on that house, but it did not fall, because it had been founded on the rock.

Roots reaching into Good Soil and a house built on solid rock are the same teaching from two angles. Both are about depth. Both withstand storms. The storms in your life are not a question of if. They are a question of when. The disciplines are how the foundation gets laid before the wind picks up.

Look across history at the men and women who walked closely with Jesus through the hardest seasons. Faithful Christians who prayed daily, lived in their Bibles, gathered with God's people, held their possessions with open hands, and fought sin honestly all the way to the end. They were not impressive in the world's eyes. They were rooted. And when sickness, suffering, persecution, or loss came for them, they displayed a depth of satisfaction in God that the world could not explain. They had built on the rock. They had grown deep before they ever needed to grow strong.
4.3 - If Jesus is inviting you to deepen one root over the next month, which one is it? What is one small daily step you could build into your week to start?
This Week's Practice

Before this week ends, take ten quiet minutes and score yourself one to ten on each of the five roots. The point is not to feel ashamed of low numbers. The point is to be honest about where you are starting. Then pick one root and one small daily practice for the next thirty days. Keep it small enough that you can actually do it. The point is not to be impressive. The point is to keep your roots drinking from Jesus, the Good Soil, day after day.
Lesson 4 Review

Jesus is your good Shepherd, good Healer, good Savior, Good Soil, and the rock the wise builder builds on. Roots in good soil and a house on solid rock are the same teaching: depth that holds when the storms come. The five roots that sink down into Him are prayer, the Word, fellowship, stewardship, and fighting sin. The deeper they go, the more of His life rises into the fruit above. In the next lesson, you begin three lessons on the Bible itself, because the Bible is one of the deepest roots you will sink into for the rest of your life.
Closing Reflections
What is one key thing you learned from this lesson?
What is one step you are going to take this week?
What is a prayer request for this week?
